1: 'Woe to Ariel, to Ariel, the city where David made his camp!' — Ariel (ari'el) is a pun-name for Jerusalem carrying two senses: (1) 'lion of God' (ari = lion + el = God) = the city of royal power; and (2) 'hearth of God' (ariel = the altar hearth of the temple, Ezekiel 43:15-16 uses ariel for the top of the altar). Isaiah exploits both meanings: Jerusalem is both YHWH's lion-city and the altar-hearth where sacrifices are offered — and the city itself will become the sacrifice. 'The city where David made his camp' (qiryah chanah david, the city David camped) = Zion, the Jebusite city David captured in 2 Samuel 5:7-9. 'Add year to year; let the festivals continue their cycle' (sefeh shanah al-shanah chaggim yingozu, add year to year, let the feasts go around) — sarcastic: continue your religious calendar uninterrupted; judgment is approaching anyway.
2: 'Yet I will besiege Ariel; there will be mourning and lamentation, and she will become to me like an altar hearth' — YHWH becomes the besieger: 'vahasiqoti la'ariel vehayeta tagon va'aniyah vehaytah li ka'ariel, I will besiege Ariel, and she will be mourning and lamentation, and she will be to me like an altar hearth.' The city named 'altar hearth' (ariel) will become an actual altar hearth — the city itself consumed in the fire of divine judgment as a sacrifice on its own altar. The mourning and lamentation (tagon va'aniyah, grief and groaning) reverse the festival-joy of the calendar prescribed in Isaiah 29:1. The bitter wordplay: the city that should be the site of sacrifices offered to YHWH becomes the sacrifice.
3: 'I will encamp against you on every side; I will besiege you with a mound and raise siege-works against you' — YHWH as the besieging army: 'vechanithi aleikh dur vestarti aleikh matsav veheqimoti aleikh metsodot, I will encamp against you as a circle, I will besiege you with a watch-post, and I will raise siege-works against you.' The 'circle' (dur = a circle, encompassing siege) = the complete surrounding of the city. The siege-mound (matsav, a raised platform for battering rams or archers) and siege-works (metsodot, siege-towers/fortifications) = standard ANE siege technology that Assyria had perfected. The theological shock: YHWH uses the same siege techniques against his own city that foreign empires use against enemy cities.
4: 'Then you will be brought low; you will speak from the ground, and your words will come up from the dust; your voice will be like a ghost murmuring from the ground, and your speech will whisper out of the dust' — the complete humiliation of the besieged: 'veshafalt min-ha'arets tedabberi, you will be brought low, from the ground you will speak.' The Hebrew word for 'brought low' (shafalt) is the same root as the Day-of-the-LORD's bringing low the proud (Isaiah 2:9, 11-12, 17). Speaking 'from the ground' and 'out of the dust' = speaking as the dead speak — the defeated city's voice is reduced to a ghost-whisper. 'Like a ghost murmuring' (ke'ob, like an ob) — the ob was the familiar spirit or ghost consulted by necromancers (Isaiah 8:19; 1 Samuel 28:7-8 = the witch of Endor's 'ob'). The city's voice reduced to the level of a spirit-consultation in the underworld.
5: 'But the horde of your enemies will become like fine dust, and the multitude of the ruthless like blowing chaff — and in an instant, suddenly —' — the sudden reversal: 'vehayah ke'avaq dak hamon zarayikh, and the horde of your enemies will become like fine dust.' The 'fine dust' (avaq dak = fine/thin dust, the finest particles that blow away instantly) and 'blowing chaff' (kamotz over, like chaff passing away) = the harvest-threshing imagery where chaff blows away in the wind (Psalms 1:4; Isaiah 35:5; Isaiah 83:13; Isaiah 17:13; Isaiah 41:2). 'In an instant, suddenly' (lepeta pith'om, in an instant, suddenly) = the characteristic vocabulary of YHWH's sudden intervention (Isaiah 28:19: 'morning by morning it will come'; Isaiah 30:13: 'suddenly, in an instant'). The dramatic three-dot pause after 'suddenly' heightens the reversal effect.
6: 'You will be visited by the LORD of hosts with thunder and earthquake and great noise, with storm and tempest and flame of devouring fire' — the theophanic intervention: 'me'im YHWH tseva'ot tippaqed bera'am uvera'ash uvaqol gadol, from YHWH of hosts you will be visited with thunder and earthquake and great noise.' The four pairs of theophanic phenomena: thunder (ra'am), earthquake (ra'ash), great noise (qol gadol), storm (sufa), tempest (sea'rah), flame of devouring fire (lehav esh okhelah). This is the Sinai theophany vocabulary (Exodus 19:16-18: thunder, lightning, thick cloud, very loud trumpet blast, smoke, fire, earthquake) applied to YHWH's eschatological intervention. YHWH the siege-engine (Isaiah 29:3) becomes YHWH the cosmic warrior who defeats the besiegers.
7: 'And the multitude of all the nations that fight against Ariel — all who fight against her and her fortifications and press her hard — will be like a dream, a vision of the night' — the sudden evaporation of the nations. 'Vehayah kehalom, and it will be like a dream' (chalom = the dream that is vivid in the moment but dissolves on waking). 'Chazon layelah, a vision of the night' — the night-vision (not a prophetic vision but a sleeping dream). All the nations who muster against Zion — with their siege-mounds and battering rams — will evaporate like a dream. Psalms 73:20: 'As a dream when one awakes, so when you arise, O Lord, you will despise them as fantasies.' The theological claim: imperial power, however overwhelming, is as substantial as a dream before YHWH's awakening.
8: 'As when a hungry man dreams of eating and wakes still hungry, or as when a thirsty man dreams of drinking and wakes still parched and craving — so will the multitude of all the nations be that fight against Mount Zion' — the simile completed: 'ka'asher yachalom hara'ev vehineh okhel vehekits vereikah nafsho, as when the hungry man dreams and behold he eats but wakes and his soul is empty.' The dream-of-eating: vivid satisfaction that vanishes on waking — the dreamer wakes with the same hunger, unrelieved by the dream-meal. 'And as when the thirsty man dreams and behold he drinks but wakes and behold he is exhausted and his soul is craving' (vehadom yachalom vehineh shote vehekits vehineh ayef venafshov shoteqah). The nations' great effort against Zion = the dream-effort: they consume enormous energy in the dream (siege warfare) but achieve nothing real.
9: 'Be stunned and bewildered; be blinded and stay blind! They are drunk, but not with wine; they stagger, but not with strong drink' — the command to be stupefied: 'hitmahmahu vatmahohu, be stunned and cause to be stunned.' The reflexive-causative form = be yourself stupefied and cause stupefaction in others. 'Be blinded' (hisha'ashu, lose control of your sight) — the total sensory disorientation. The crucial distinction: 'lo yayin shiggegah, not from wine they are drunk; lo shekhar yanu'u, not from strong drink they stagger.' The drunkenness is spiritual: the same drunkenness as the corrupt priests of Isaiah 28:7, now applied more broadly. The paradox: their disorientation is YHWH's judgment on their refusal to receive his revelation (Isaiah 29:11-12).
10: 'For the LORD has poured out on you a spirit of deep sleep; he has shut your eyes — the prophets — and covered your heads — the seers' — the divine inducement of spiritual incapacity: 'ki nasakh aleikhem YHWH ruach tardema, for YHWH has poured over you a spirit of deep sleep' (tardema = the deep sleep of Genesis 2:21 where YHWH caused Adam's deep sleep to form Eve; Genesis 15:12 where the deep sleep came upon Abram before the covenant-cutting ceremony). The deep sleep as divine action before decisive divine activity. 'Vaye'atser et-eineikhem et-hanevi'im, he has closed your eyes — the prophets.' The seers (harozim, those who see/prophesy) have their heads covered (kisah). Paul quotes Isaiah 29:10 in Romans 11:8: 'God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that could not see and ears that could not hear, to this very day.'
11: 'And the vision of all this has become to you like the words of a sealed book' — the inaccessibility of divine revelation: 'vattehi lakhem chazut hakol kedivre hassefer hechatumm, and the vision of all this has become to you like the words of a sealed document.' The 'sealed book' (sefer chatumm, document sealed with wax seal) = a legal document sealed and therefore inaccessible until officially opened by the authorized party. 'When someone gives it to a literate person... I cannot — it is sealed' — the literate person (yode'a sefer, one who knows a book = the scribal-educated) cannot read a sealed document, even though he has the skill. The seal must be broken by the legitimate authority. YHWH's revelation is inaccessible — not because it was not given, but because the recipients are spiritually sealed from receiving it.
12: 'And when the book is given to one who cannot read, saying, Please read this, he answers, I cannot read' — the second scenario: the illiterate person (asher lo-yada sefer, one who does not know a book) cannot read even an unsealed book. The two cases together create a complete circle of inaccessibility: the literate cannot read because it is sealed; the illiterate cannot read because he lacks the skill. The divine word — present, given, potentially readable — is completely inaccessible to a people under the 'spirit of deep sleep.' Cf. Isaiah 6:9-10: 'hearing but never understanding, seeing but never perceiving.' The same diagnostic of prophetic failure-of-reception that appears throughout Isaiah and is quoted by Jesus (Matthew 13:14-15) and Paul (Acts 28:26-27; Romans 11:8).
13: 'Because this people draw near with their mouth and honor me with their lips, while their heart is far from me, and their reverence for me is merely a commandment learned by rote from men' — the diagnosis of hollow religion. 'Ya'an ki niggash ha'am hazeh bephiv uvisfataiv kibbeduini, because this people draw near with their mouth and with their lips they honor me.' The contrast: the mouth/lips (external religious speech) vs. the heart (libbo, the seat of will, intention, and understanding). 'Their reverence for me is merely a commandment of men learned by rote' (vetihi yir'atam oti mitsvat anashim melumadah, and their fear of me is a commandment of men learned by habit/rote). The mitsvat anashim (commandment of men) vs. the mitsvot of YHWH = the religious tradition that has replaced the living covenant relationship. Jesus quotes this in Matthew 15:8-9 and Mark 7:6-7, applying it to Pharisaic tradition that nullified the Torah.
14: 'Therefore, watch — I am about to do something astonishing among this people, something astonishing and astounding; the wisdom of their wise men will perish and the insight of their discerning men will vanish' — the divine subversion of elite wisdom: 'lakhen hineni yosif lehapli et-ha'am hazeh hafle vafele, therefore I am adding to do wonders to this people, a wonder and a wonder' (hafle vafele = wonder and wonder, double emphatic for the astonishing). The 'wisdom of their wise men will perish' (veavedah chokhmah chakhamav, and the wisdom of their wise men will be lost) = the political-religious intelligentsia whose advice led to the Egypt alliance and rejection of prophetic counsel. Paul quotes Isaiah 29:14 in 1 Corinthians 1:19: 'For it is written: I will destroy the wisdom of the wise; the intelligence of the intelligent I will frustrate' — applied to the cross as the definitive 'astonishing thing' that confounds human wisdom.
15: 'Woe to those who go to great lengths to hide their plans from the LORD, who work in darkness and say, Who sees us? Who knows us?' — the woe against concealment from YHWH: 'hoi hamme'ammikim me'YHWH lasstir etsah, woe to those who deepen from YHWH to hide their counsel' (hamme'ammikim = those who go deep = those who dig deeply to conceal). 'Who work in darkness' (uvechoshekh ma'aseihem, in darkness their deeds) — the darkness as the arena of conspiracy and political plotting. 'Who says, Who sees us? Who knows us?' (mi ro'enu umi yodenu, who sees us, who knows us?) — the rhetorical denial of divine omniscience. This is the sin of practical atheism: the political counselors who conduct their Egypt alliance negotiations without reference to YHWH because they functionally deny his oversight. Psalms 94:7-9: 'They say, The Lord does not see; the God of Jacob takes no notice... Does he who formed the eye not see?'
16: 'How you have everything backwards! Should the potter be regarded as no different from clay? Should what is made say of its maker, He did not make me? Should what is formed say of him who formed it, He has no understanding?' — the potter-clay reversal. 'Haphkhem, your turning upside-down!' (hafkham = your inversion, your complete reversal of the proper order) — not just a question but an exclamation of the absurdity. The clay (chomer) asking whether the potter (yotser, the one who forms = YHWH who 'formed' Adam in Genesis 2:7) should be treated as clay = the creature treating the Creator as an equal object. 'Should what is formed say of him who formed it, He has no understanding?' (veyetser amar leyotsero lo hevin, and the formed-thing says to its former, he has no understanding) = the creature claiming intellectual superiority over the Creator. Paul uses the potter-clay in Romans 9:20-21 to address election: 'Does what is formed say to its former, Why did you make me like this?'
17: 'Is it not just a little while until Lebanon becomes a fruitful orchard, and the fruitful orchard is considered a forest?' — the eschatological reversal of natural orders: 'halo od me'at miz'ar veshav Levanon lakkarmel, is it not a very little while, and Lebanon will turn into a fruitful field?' Lebanon (the cedar forest = the wild, unproductive but majestic forest) becomes karmel (the fruitful orchard, the cultivated productive land). Karmel (the orchard) is considered ya'ar (forest = the wild unproductive land). The reversal: the wild becomes cultivated, the cultivated is now in the category of wild. The theological application: the 'little while' (me'at miz'ar, very small from small) before the reversal of all current categories. This sets up Isaiah 29:18-21: the deaf hear, the blind see, the meek rejoice — all the current 'wild' (unproductive) categories become 'cultivated' (productive).
18: 'In that day the deaf will hear the words of a book, and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see' — the healing of the sealed-book incapacity: 'veshamu vayom hahu hacherashim divrei sefer, and the deaf will hear in that day the words of the book.' The sealed book of Isaiah 29:11 (which neither literate nor illiterate could access) will be heard by the deaf. The 'words of a book' (divrei sefer = the prophetic scroll) = the revelation that was inaccessible under the spirit of deep sleep (Isaiah 29:10). 'And out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see' (ume'afela umichoshekh einei ivrim tir'einah, and from dimness and darkness the eyes of the blind will see). The reversal of spiritual blindness (Isaiah 29:10: the prophets' eyes shut). Jesus quotes this reversal in Matthew 11:5 as evidence of messianic fulfillment: 'the blind receive sight, the deaf hear.'
19: 'The meek will find renewed joy in the LORD, and the poorest among people will exult in the Holy One of Israel' — the reversal of social deprivation: 'veyasfu anavim simchah baYHWH, and the meek/humble will add joy in YHWH' (yasfu = they will add/increase joy, from yasaf = to add). The anavim (the meek/humble = the poor-and-afflicted, both economically poor and spiritually humble) are the primary beneficiaries of the eschatological reversal. 'The poorest among people' (evyone adam = the poor of humanity, the destitute in the most absolute sense) 'will exult in the Holy One of Israel' (yagilu biqedosh Yisrael, will rejoice in the Holy One of Israel). The Holy One of Israel (qedosh Yisrael) — Isaiah's distinctive divine title (25 occurrences in Isaiah vs. 6 elsewhere in OT). The meek-rejoicing echoes Psalms 37:11: 'the meek will inherit the land and enjoy peace and prosperity' — Jesus quotes this in the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:5).
20: 'For the tyrant will be no more, and the scoffer will vanish, and all who look for opportunity to do wrong will be cut off' — the elimination of the oppressors: 'ki-afel arits, for the violent oppressor will be gone' (afel = gone/finished, from afes = to cease). The three categories removed: arits (the violent/ruthless, the tyrant), lets (the scoffer — the same word as the men of latson/scoffing who were Jerusalem's rulers in Isaiah 28:14-22), and tsofe le'awon (those watching for evil = those who look for legal or political opportunities to harm others). The removal of these three from the eschatological community = the restoration of the covenant social order where the poor and meek are no longer preyed upon.
21: 'Those who trap a man with a word, who lay snares for the one who rebukes corruption at the gate, and who use empty claims to deny justice to the innocent' — the three specific legal-oppression techniques of the scoffer-class: (1) 'machati adam bedavar, who make a man a sinner by a word' = legal entrapment, using a man's own words to bring false charges (cf. Matthew 22:15: the Pharisees tried to 'trap him in his words'); (2) 'ulamoqesh mokhiach sha'ar, who lay a snare for the one who reproves at the gate' = targeting the judge or advocate who tries to correct corruption at the gate-court (the city gate was the legal forum: Ruth 4:1-12; Amos 5:10-12: 'they hate the one who reproves in the gate'); (3) 'vayattu vetohu tsaddiq, and turn aside with emptiness the just' = using false legal arguments to deny justice to innocent parties.
22: 'Therefore thus says the LORD, who redeemed Abraham, concerning the house of Jacob: Jacob will no longer be ashamed; his face will no longer grow pale' — the Abraham-redemption foundation of the promise: 'lakhen ko-amar YHWH el-beit Ya'aqov asher padah et-Avraham, therefore thus says YHWH to the house of Jacob, who redeemed Abraham.' YHWH as 'the one who redeemed Abraham' (padah et-Avraham) — the call from Ur (Genesis 12:1-3) as YHWH's 'redemption' of Abraham from Mesopotamian idolatry. This is the only place in the OT where YHWH is said to have 'redeemed' Abraham specifically. The foundation: if YHWH could redeem Abraham from paganism and build a nation, he can restore Jacob from shame. 'Jacob will no longer be ashamed' (lo-yevosh Ya'aqov, Jacob will not be ashamed) — the shame of national defeat and exile reversed.
23: 'For when he sees his children — the work of my hands — in his midst, they will sanctify my name; they will sanctify the Holy One of Jacob and stand in awe of the God of Israel' — the eschatological community sanctifying YHWH: 'ki bire'oto yeladaiv ma'assei yadai beqirbo yaqdishu shemi, for when he sees his children, the work of my hands, in his midst, they will sanctify my name.' The 'children' (yeladaiv, his children = the restored community) are simultaneously 'the work of my hands' (ma'assei yadai = YHWH's own handiwork). The sanctifying of YHWH's name (yaqdishu shemi) echoes the Third Commandment and the LORD's Prayer ('hallowed be your name'). 'The Holy One of Jacob' (qedosh Ya'aqov) — a rare variant of the usual 'Holy One of Israel,' emphasizing the covenant relationship going back to the patriarch Jacob.
24: 'Those who went astray in spirit will come to understanding, and those who complained will accept instruction' — the restoration of the spiritually wandering: 'veyade'u to'ei-ruach vinah, and those who erred in spirit will know understanding.' The to'ei-ruach (those who wandered in spirit = the spiritually disoriented, those whose inner inclination was lost) will 'know' (yade'u, from yada = the covenant-knowledge of intimate relationship). 'And those who complained will accept instruction' (verogenim yilmadu leqach, and those who murmured will learn instruction) — the rogenim (the grumblers, the same word as Israel's wilderness murmuring in Deuteronomy 1:27; Psalms 106:25) will 'learn leqach' (leqach = teaching, the distilled wisdom that can be received). The full community restoration: both the spiritually wandering and the complaining resisters are brought to understanding and teachability.
